items.q: In a standard hydrogen electrode used as a reference, what is the assigned electrode potential?
items.correct: Exactly 0 V by convention — all other electrode potentials are measured relative to it.
items.misconception: The standard hydrogen electrode actually has zero potential because hydrogen has no charge.
items.random_wrong: The standard hydrogen electrode has a potential of -1 V to balance copper at +1 V.
items.why: Students don't realise 0 V is a chosen reference, not a measured value; they think hydrogen is genuinely neutral electrochemically.

items.correct: A substance made of only one type of atom
items.why: It's A substance made of only one type of atom. “A mixture of metals” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Which of these is the correct symbol for sodium?
items.why: It's Na. “So” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Approximately how many elements are on the modern periodic table?
items.why: Exact answer: 118. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
